---
layout: default
title: RSA - Lee Cooper
---

---
## Rootme - RSA - Lee Cooper
---
## Description

During a digital investigation, I managed to extract a lot of files including one that seemed rather interesting. Lacking time, I did not attempt to review its contents in depth but I’m quite sure it contains vital information. I’m counting on you to extract it !

---
## Solution

We can use the Coopersmith short pad attack and Franklin-Reiter Related Message Attack to solve this challenge.

The combination of these two techniques helps us to find the message $$m$$ when we have
{%raw%}
$$c_1 \equiv (m+r_1)^e \ mod \ n, \quad  c_2 \equiv (m+r_2)^e \ mod \ n,$$
{%endraw%}
with $$r_1,r_2$$ unknown.

This is the exact scenario we are dealing with, since 
{%raw%}
$$c_1 \equiv (part1+q_1)^7 \ mod \ n_1, \quad  c_2 \equiv (part1+q_2)^7 \ mod \ n_1,$$
{%endraw%}
where $$q_1$$ and $$q_2$$ are unknown, and anagously with part2. Therefore, we can recover part1 and part2 by implementing this approach. I highly recommend reading the paper *20 years of Attacks on the RSA Cryptosystem*, where these techniques are explained and proven.

Once we have part1 and part2, let $$int(x)$$ be the function that encodes strings into integers, for example, the bytes_to_long function from PyCryptodome. Notice that this function satisfies
{%raw%}
$$int(s_1+s_2) = 256^{len(s_2)}int(s_1)+ int(s_2).$$
{%endraw%}

The value $$c_5$$ is computed as 
{%raw%}
$$c_5 \equiv int(w+p_1+p_2+f+e)^5 \ mod \ n,$$
{%endraw%} 
where $$w$$ is the welcome string, $$p_1$$ and $$p_2$$ are the part1 and part2 found previously, $$f$$ is the flag, and $$e$$ is the end string.

Therefore,
{%raw%}
$$\begin{align*}
c_5 &\equiv (int(w+p_1+p_2+f+e))^5 \ mod \ n\\
&\equiv (256^{len(e)} int(w+p_1+p_2+f)+int(e))^5 \ mod \ n \\
&\equiv (256^{len(e)+len(f)}int(w+p_1+p_2)+256^{len(e)}int(f) +int(e))^5 \ mod \ n.
\end{align*}$$
{%endraw%}

Thus,
{%raw%}
$$\begin{align*}
c_5256^{-5len(e)} &\equiv (256^{len(f)}int(w+p_1+p_2)+256^{-len(e)}int(e)+int(f))^5 \ mod \ n
\end{align*}.$$
{%endraw%}

We can then construct the polynomial
{%raw%}
$$f(x) = (x+b)^5-c \ mod \ n,$$
{%endraw%}
where
{%raw%}
$$\begin{align*}
b &= 256^{len(f)}int(w+p_1+p_2)+256^{-len(e)}int(e), \\
c &= c_5256^{-5len(e)},
\end{align*}$$
{%endraw%}

and find the root.

Since we do not know the length of the flag, we can iterate on $$len(f)$$ until a solution is found.

*SageMath script*
```python
from Crypto.Util.number import bytes_to_long, long_to_bytes



def coppersmith_short_pad(C1, C2, N, e, eps = 1/25):
    P.<x, y> = PolynomialRing(Zmod(N))
    P2.<y> = PolynomialRing(Zmod(N))

    g1 = (x^e - C1).change_ring(P2)
    g2 = ((x + y)^e - C2).change_ring(P2)
 
    res = g1.resultant(g2, variable=x)

    roots = res.univariate_polynomial().change_ring(Zmod(N)).small_roots(epsilon=eps)
    
    return roots[0]
    
def franklin_reiter(C1, C2, N, r, e):
    P.<x> = PolynomialRing(Zmod(N))

    g1 = x^e - C1
    g2 = (x+r)^e - C2

    def gcd(g1, g2):
        while g2:
            g1, g2 = g2, g1 % g2
        return g1.monic()

    return -gcd(g1, g2)[0]

n1    = 
n     = 
c1    = 
c2    = 
c3    = 
c4    = 
c5    = 



diff = coppersmith_short_pad(c1, c2, n1, e =7, eps=1/80)
part1 = franklin_reiter(c1, c2, n1, diff, e=7)

diff = coppersmith_short_pad(c3, c4, n1, e =7, eps=1/80)
part2 = franklin_reiter(c3, c4, n1, diff, e=7)

 
welcome = "Welcome ! This challenge is supposed to be complex but I'm pretty sure that you will find the flag in a sec.\n"
end     = "\nI hope you enjoyed the challenge !!"
part1 = long_to_bytes(int(part1)).decode(errors='ignore').split('|')[1]
part2 = long_to_bytes(int(part2)).decode(errors='ignore').split('|')[1]


for k in range(1,100):

    b = pow(256,k,n)*bytes_to_long( (welcome+part1+part2).encode()) + pow(256,-len(end),n)*bytes_to_long(end.encode())
    P.<x> = PolynomialRing(Zmod(n))
    f = (x + b)^5 - c5*pow(256,-5*len(end),n)
    roots = f.small_roots(256**(k+1),eps=1/20)

    if len(roots) == 1:
        flag = int(roots[0])
        print(long_to_bytes(flag).decode())
        break


```
